content.text: Every chunk stored in a vector store carries two things: a vector and a payload. The
is a dict of metadata fields that travel with the chunk from parse time through retrieval to the final generated answer. The
is the unique key used to upsert, fetch, and delete that chunk. Getting both right is what enables source citations, metadata filtering, idempotent re-indexing, and selective deletion.

attrs.alt: Metadata lifecycle: Source document → parse and attach metadata → chunk (add chunk_index and chunk_id) → embed and store (id = chunk_id) → vector store. At query time: pre-filter by metadata before ANN search, post-filter from results, surface source/page in citations, idempotent upsert via SHA-256 IDs.

content.text: Metadata serves four distinct roles in a RAG system. Skipping any one of them forces costly workarounds later:
source + page_number surface in the LLM prompt so the model can cite "annual_report.pdf p.4" in its answer
doc_type, created_at, department narrow the ANN search to the relevant subset before scoring (pre-filter) or after (post-filter)
Delete all chunks for a document when it is removed: filter by source = "old_report.pdf" and delete matching IDs
chunk_index + source lets you reconstruct which passage the retriever returned and whether the answer faithfully cites it
Design the metadata schema once at the loader stage. These fields cover the vast majority of RAG use cases:
Filename or URL — the single most important field for citations and deletion
PDF page (1-indexed); DOCX heading level as fallback; blank for web pages
earnings_release, policy, faq, ticket — enables filtered queries
Document publication or creation date — filter out stale knowledge
Route to language-specific index or embedding model
Heading the chunk falls under — improves context when cited
Position of this chunk within the parent document — used in chunk_id
Deterministic unique key; enables idempotent upserts and precise deletion
Add only fields you will actually filter on or surface in citations. Extra keys increase storage and add noise. A flat dict with 5–8 fields is easier to maintain than 20.
. Set all document-level fields here, before splitting:

from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
from pathlib import Path
import datetime

def load_with_metadata(
    filepath: str,
    doc_type: str,
    created_at: str | None = None,
) -> list:
    """Load a PDF and enrich every page-Document with structured metadata."""
    loader = PyPDFLoader(filepath)
    docs = loader.load()

    source_name = Path(filepath).name
    date_str = created_at or datetime.date.today().isoformat()

    for doc in docs:
        doc.metadata.update({
            "source":      source_name,
            "doc_type":    doc_type,
            "created_at":  date_str,
            "language":    "en",        # or detect via langdetect
            # page_number is already set by PyPDFLoader as doc.metadata["page"]
            "page_number": doc.metadata.get("page", 0) + 1,  # convert to 1-indexed
        })

    return docs

docs = load_with_metadata(
    "annual_report_2025.pdf",
    doc_type="earnings_release",
    created_at="2025-10-15",
)
print(docs[0].metadata)
# {'source': 'annual_report_2025.pdf', 'page': 0, 'doc_type': 'earnings_release',
#  'created_at': '2025-10-15', 'language': 'en', 'page_number': 1}

When a document has headings (DOCX, HTML, Markdown), carry the current section title into each chunk's metadata. The title rarely appears verbatim in the chunk text because the splitter cuts across paragraph boundaries — but it is invaluable for citations and for the LLM to understand the passage's context.

import re
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Track the current heading while iterating pages
def split_with_section_titles(
    raw_text: str,
    source: str,
    page_number: int,
    base_metadata: dict,
    chunk_size: int = 512,
    chunk_overlap: int = 64,
) -> list[Document]:
    from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

    HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^(#+\s+|[A-Z][A-Z 0-9]{5,}$)", re.M)

    # Find all headings in this page's text and their character positions
    headings = [(m.start(), m.group().strip("# ").strip())
                for m in HEADING_RE.finditer(raw_text)]

    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=chunk_size, chunk_overlap=chunk_overlap
    )
    chunks = splitter.create_documents([raw_text])

    # Assign each chunk the heading that precedes its start position
    current_heading = "Introduction"
    h_idx = 0
    char_pos = 0
    result = []
    for chunk in chunks:
        # advance heading cursor as we move through the text
        chunk_start = raw_text.find(chunk.page_content[:40], char_pos)
        while h_idx < len(headings) and headings[h_idx][0] <= chunk_start:
            current_heading = headings[h_idx][1]
            h_idx += 1
        chunk.metadata = {
            **base_metadata,
            "source":        source,
            "page_number":   page_number,
            "section_title": current_heading,
        }
        char_pos = chunk_start + len(chunk.page_content)
        result.append(chunk)

    return result

Every chunk needs a stable, deterministic ID. Using a deterministic hash of the chunk's source position means re-indexing the same document produces the same IDs — so the vector store upserts overwrite existing vectors rather than creating duplicates. Random UUIDs break this guarantee.

import hashlib

def make_chunk_id(source: str, chunk_index: int) -> str:
    """Stable ID: same source + position → same ID every time."""
    key = f"{source}::{chunk_index}"
    return hashlib.sha256(key.encode()).hexdigest()

# Example
print(make_chunk_id("annual_report_2025.pdf", 11))
# 'c8f3a1b9e2d5f4a7b3c9d1e6f8a2b4c7d0e3f5a8b1c4d7e0f2a5b8c1d4e7f0a3'

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.documents import Document
import hashlib

def chunk_with_ids(docs: list[Document]) -> list[Document]:
    """Split documents and assign a deterministic chunk_id to every chunk."""
    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)
    chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

    # Group chunks by source to assign per-source chunk_index
    from collections import defaultdict
    source_counters: dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)

    for chunk in chunks:
        source = chunk.metadata.get("source", "unknown")
        idx = source_counters[source]
        source_counters[source] += 1

        chunk_id = hashlib.sha256(f"{source}::{idx}".encode()).hexdigest()
        chunk.metadata["chunk_index"] = idx
        chunk.metadata["chunk_id"]    = chunk_id

    return chunks

# Usage
chunks = chunk_with_ids(docs)
print(chunks[0].metadata)
# {'source': 'annual_report_2025.pdf', 'doc_type': 'earnings_release',
#  'page_number': 1, 'chunk_index': 0, 'chunk_id': 'a3b7c9...'}
An idempotent upsert writes a chunk if it does not exist, or updates it if it does — identified by its
. This lets you re-run the indexing pipeline when a document changes without accumulating duplicate vectors.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

# Pinecone example — upsert uses chunk_id as the vector ID
import pinecone

def upsert_chunks(chunks, index_name: str = "my-index"):
    pc = pinecone.Pinecone()
    index = pc.Index(index_name)
    emb = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

    batch_size = 100
    for i in range(0, len(chunks), batch_size):
        batch = chunks[i:i + batch_size]
        texts   = [c.page_content for c in batch]
        vectors = emb.embed_documents(texts)

        records = [
            {
                "id":     c.metadata["chunk_id"],   # deterministic ID → upsert
                "values": vec,
                "metadata": {
                    "text":          c.page_content,   # stored for retrieval
                    "source":        c.metadata["source"],
                    "page_number":   c.metadata.get("page_number"),
                    "doc_type":      c.metadata.get("doc_type"),
                    "created_at":    c.metadata.get("created_at"),
                    "chunk_index":   c.metadata.get("chunk_index"),
                },
            }
            for c, vec in zip(batch, vectors)
        ]
        index.upsert(vectors=records)
        print(f"Upserted {len(records)} vectors")

upsert_chunks(chunks)

# Delete all chunks belonging to a specific document
def delete_document(source: str, index_name: str = "my-index"):
    pc = pinecone.Pinecone()
    index = pc.Index(index_name)

    # Fetch IDs by metadata filter (Pinecone supports this directly)
    results = index.query(
        vector=[0.0] * 1536,   # dummy vector — we only need IDs
        filter={"source": {"$eq": source}},
        top_k=10_000,
        include_metadata=False,
    )
    ids = [m["id"] for m in results["matches"]]

    if ids:
        index.delete(ids=ids)
        print(f"Deleted {len(ids)} chunks for {source}")

delete_document("old_report_q2_2025.pdf")
dict alongside the ANN query. Only vectors matching the filter participate in the ANN search (pre-filter) or are returned (post-filter). The filter syntax varies by store:
Filter the result list in Python after .search()
The generator prompt should include source metadata alongside each chunk so the LLM can cite it. Format the context block to make the source obvious:

def format_docs_with_citations(docs) -> str:
    """Render retrieved chunks with inline source references for the LLM."""
    parts = []
    for doc in docs:
        m = doc.metadata
        source_label = m.get("source", "unknown")
        page = m.get("page_number")
        section = m.get("section_title", "")

        if page:
            ref = f"[{source_label} p.{page}]"
        else:
            ref = f"[{source_label}]"
        if section:
            ref += f" — {section}"

        parts.append(f"{ref}
{doc.page_content}")
    return "

".join(parts)

# Prompt template instructs the model to cite
SYSTEM = (
    "Answer using ONLY the context passages below. "
    "For every claim, cite the source label in brackets, e.g. [annual_report_2025.pdf p.4]."
)

context = format_docs_with_citations(results)
# context =
# [annual_report_2025.pdf p.4] — Financial Highlights
# Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1B, up 18% year-over-year...
#
# [annual_report_2025.pdf p.5] — Segment Performance
# Cloud segment grew 34% driven by enterprise adoption...
Re-indexing creates duplicates; vector count grows unboundedly
Use SHA-256 of (source + chunk_index) for deterministic IDs
Answers have no citations; cannot delete by document
Always set metadata["source"] at load time
Storage bloat; harder to reason about filter logic
Keep to 5–8 fields; only add what you will filter or cite
Using FAISS and expecting metadata filter
No filter support; all chunks scored even if doc_type wrong
Post-filter in Python, or switch to Chroma / Pinecone / Weaviate
Citations say "p.0" instead of "p.1" for the first page
Add 1 to PyPDFLoader's 0-indexed page field when setting page_number
Chunks have no heading context; LLM cannot tell what a number refers to
Track current heading during splitting and add to each chunk's metadata
Metadata is set at load time and travels with every chunk through splitting, embedding, and storage.
The minimum useful fields are: source, page_number, doc_type, created_at, chunk_index, chunk_id.
Use SHA-256 of (source + chunk_index) as chunk_id to make upserts idempotent and enable precise deletion.
Pre-filter metadata before ANN search for speed; post-filter from results when the filter is applied after retrieval.
Propagate section_title through the splitter so each chunk knows which heading it falls under.
Format context blocks with inline source labels so the LLM can cite [filename p.N] in its answer.
FAISS has no native metadata filter — use Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector if filtering is required.
